comments.text: We have written a proposed resolution to the remaining limsup question in Erdos Problem 1054. Let \(f(N)\) be the least positive integer \(m\) such that \(N\) is the sum of the \(k\) smallest positive divisors of \(m\), for some \(k\geq 1\). We prove that, for every fixed \(A\geq 1\), the set of \(N\) satisfying\[ f(N)>AN \]has positive lower density. Consequently,\[ \limsup_{N\to\infty}\frac{f(N)}{N}=\infty. \]Here is the latex solution, as well as the Lean formalization of our claim. The Lean file isolates two classical analytic inputs that do not appear to be available in Mathlib: a quantitative Mertens product estimate and an almost-all binary Goldbach theorem. These are stated as explicit assumptions rather than formalized from first principles. Note: we recently learned of jif's proof that every positive integer other than \(2\) and \(5\) is representable. His argument handles a large finite range by explicit computation and prime-window interval extension, and uses an explicit consequence of Helfgott's ternary Goldbach theorem for the remaining tail. Our current Lean formalization instead uses an almost-all binary Goldbach estimate, since we were not aware of jif's argument when it was written. This work was carried out using Principia Math, a frontier model harness & open-source map of mathematics that my team and I are building. We're currently in beta testing. Most of the final push was carried out by GPT-5.5 Pro, with assistance from other frontier models, especially Claude Opus 4.8. We have tried to cite all relevant prior work and would appreciate being told about any omissions. We're incredibly grateful to everyone who contributed to the solution of this problem. It's been very fun to work on.
Sorry, I did no work related to this problem, but was cited for various achievements in the paper. This doesn't speak well to the accuracy of your Principia Math pipeline.

Thank you for pointing this out and point taken. I put together the citations yesterday and somehow came to the conclusion that you and jif were the same user, but this appears to not be the case. Apologies, and we will fix this as soon as possible.

This should now be fixed. Thanks again for pointing this out. Jif, I'm not sure if this is your first name. Let us know how you would prefer to be cited and we will be sure to update the solution tex as well as the lean verification.

It would perhaps make sense to unify all related work on this problem into a single paper, starting with Terry's disproof of $f(n)=o(n)$, but also realizing that certain ideas and tricks are shared among different parts of the solution and avoiding repetitions. Namely, I doubt that anyone involved published or plans to publish any of their observations, since every one of them, by itself, is probably too small to be published separately. Hopefully, most of this work could be automated, since that work might be off-putting for human mathematicians. I don't push anyone towards publishing, I don't really care. It just might be nice to have a single meaningful readable document that fully solves all aspects of this problem.

I just asked ChatGPT 5.5: look through all (partial) proofs in this thread and combine them in one clean tex file. output also as pdf. Give proper references to the authors or their nicknames. https://www.erdosproblems.com/forum/thread/1054 The answer of ChatGPT is here, as pdf and as tex file. It is not meant as solution to Vjeko's request, but instead as a possible working base. I do not take guarantees for correctness or completeness. pdf tex file Feel free to use the stuff without quoting me. I was only the prompt-man.

I do have some comments for contributions of this kind. It's 10 pages of semi-repetitive, semi-trustworthy math. It's not particularly optimized, especially for correctness or completeness. If one wants to have a base, why would one use this rather than the forum comments themselves? So not to dismiss or anything, but for me - the thought process runs something like this. I want to see if there's something to build on or be informationally useful in the writeup. After some skimming, it appears that there's some organized information, but I have to think and judge that it may be more cumbersome to extract value from it than from the initial forum comments. So some time is spent here only to conclude that I shouldn't use it. It's somewhat related to what Tao used to say about things like this not really being "digestion", which may be understood as information that's transformed usefully in some sense. Currently, I would say AI cannot reliably judge whether a certain information transformation has high or low value; I always have to supply that judgment. (So interestingly - a claim like "AI cannot write well" is not quite accurate. It's more that "AI cannot discern good writing". If you supply the discernment, AI can write much better in many cases, which is my usual strategy.) Not to say it's bad, though. But I do think it's not as useful as you might have hoped. (If someone else can use it better than me, do feel free to correct me!)

We've written a lean formalization of the entire solution to Erdos 1054, still conditional on two classic inputs that might be a bit challenging to formalize: Mertens' third theorem and an almost-all binary Goldbach theorem. There are three of us on the Principia team. Assuming every aspect of the proof holds up, we would love to coauthor a preprint of the solution with everyone who has contributed. Let us know if this is something you'd be interested in and we'd be happy to connect. In the meantime, we will work on cleaning up and simplifying the limsup argument to make it more digestible. Many thanks to everyone for your helpful comments. In particular, we're grateful to Nat for running a preliminary check on the limsup argument.

A few months ago I formalized a version of Mertens' third theorem that can seemingly be used in your formalization as well. You can find it here. Furthermore, as written 8 days ago here, Mertens' theorems should actually be available in Mathlib shortly.

This seems to be formalizing the bound\[ \#\{ N\leq X : f(N)\leq \delta N \} = O(\delta^3 X). \]Was that a deliberate choice? Between Terry's original bound $O(\delta^2 X)$ and the best one available in the comments $O(\exp(-e^{(1/\delta)^c})X)$.

We tried to formalize just enough to verify the full solution to the problem as stated (conditional on the two classic inputs, for now). I realize that some very interesting results are currently absent from the formalization. We'll expand and finalize the formalization once we have a preprint with everyone's results presented in the way they prefer. For now, if there's any aspect anyone wants us to formalize, let us know and we'd be happy to help out with this.

I have run a screening check; no issue is found. The Lean is correct (so the result is very likely correct). The Lean corresponds to the paper, and no issue is found in the paper (so the paper is plausibly correct). No strong overlap is found in literature, but this check is only preliminary evidence.

Since there have been many comments scattered around (thanks Thomas for moving them all here), I wrote up a short self-contained proof that the fraction of the numbers $n\in[1,x]$ satisfying $f(n)\leq \delta n$ is $\ll_M \delta^M$ for every $M>0$ (uniformly in $x>0$). [EDIT: After Thomas's comment, this is now improved to quite rapid decay $\ll \exp(-e^{(1/\delta)^c})$ for some small $c>0$.] It is based on Terry's proof, so all credit goes to him. This is just a minor modification and even a simplification - no decay in $k$ estimate is shown first. (No AI was used. Note that Terry's comment is from Nov 2025.)

Thanks Vjeko! (Sorry for not moving the comments earlier, back in November this feature didn't exist and only noticed the misplaced comments today.) If I'm looking at this right, I believe that your argument proves the estimate\[\ll \delta^{O(\log\log(1/\delta))}.\](The only thing that needs to be checked is the dependence on $q$ in the estimate $\sum_{n\geq 1}\frac{\tau(n)^q}{n^2}\ll_q1$, which you can bound by e.g.\[\sum \frac{\tau(n)^q}{n^2}\leq \sum_n \frac{\tau_{2^q}(n)}{n^2}=\zeta(2)^{2^q}.\]Choosing $q=c\log\log(1/\delta)$ for some small constant $c>0$ ensures this loss is at most $\delta^{-1}$, say.)

That's actually a very good point: to carefully check the dependence on $\delta$. There is another loss in the constant (in terms of $q$) coming from $\sum_{c\geq b} c^{-1-1/q} \ll q b^{-1/q}$. Still, I will more carefully check the dependence on $\delta$ and revise the note.
I think that's fine, just a loss of $q^{O(q)}$, so negligible compared to the doubly exponential loss in $q$ from the $\tau$ sum.

You are right, but it starts to matter if one wants an even better bound. Speaking of optimization, I believe that I can further optimize the proof to $\ll \exp(-e^{(1/\delta)^c})$ for some small $c>0$. I'll update the note tomorrow. [For comparison, ChatGPT from Liam's post gave the weaker (larger) bound $\ll \exp(-1/\delta-(1/2)\log(1/\delta))$.]

I updated the short note to give the quantitatively stronger result (motivated by Thomas's comment and promised above): The fraction of the numbers $n\in[1,x]$ satisfying $f(n)\leq \delta n$ is $\ll \exp(-e^{(1/\delta)^c})$ for some small constant $c>0$ and for all sufficiently small $\delta>0$, uniformly in $x>0$. I also now rather prefer to talk about the uniform fraction bound\[ \sup_{x>0}\frac{\#\{n\in[1,x] \,:\, f(n)\leq \delta n\}}{x} \ll \exp(-e^{(1/\delta)^c}) \]rather than the asymptotic upper density\[ \limsup_{x\to\infty}\frac{\#\{n\in[1,x] \,:\, f(n)\leq \delta n\}}{x} \ll \exp(-e^{(1/\delta)^c}). \]Before I thought that the upper density is the right way of quantifying the failure of Erdos's conjecture, but I'm no longer sure. The former is a stronger property (and it is safer to formulate the result that way), as the density could theoretically be $0$. However, I don't know how to prove/disprove this for every small $\delta>0$, and I don't even have an opinion on whether the density should really be $0$ or not for every sufficiently small $\delta>0$. Experiments based on the table of values of f are also a bit indecisive: For $\delta=1/2$ and for $x=1000, 5000, 10000, 20000$ the fractions are: $0.0830, 0.0670, 0.0602, 0.05625$ and it is unclear if this goes to $0$ or stabilizes at a positive number. For $\delta=1/10$ and for $x=1000, 5000, 10000, 20000$ the decay seems more convincing, as the fractions are now $0.0020, 0.0004, 0.0002, 0.0001$, but it might only be the case that these numbers stabilize much later (necessarily to a much smaller quantity). For large $\delta$ (probably already $\delta>1$) it should be easy to see that the upper density is in fact strictly positive. Perhaps this is to be added if the whole thing starts converging to some paper. Speaking of large ratios $f(n)/n$, the $\limsup$ part of the original question is also something to think about. EDIT: A brave conjecture would be that the distributions of the ratios $f(n)/n$ for $n\in[1,x]$ converge to some limit distribution as $x\to\infty$. An even braver conjecture is that the support of this limit distribution is the whole half-line $[0,\infty)$. Its approximation with $x=20000$ then looks like this (drawn in Wolfram Mathematica 13). Both of the above claims (positive density of $f(n)\leq\delta n$ for every $\delta>0$ and $\limsup f(n)/n=\infty$) would be consequences of this conjecture.

(This comment has been moved here from 468.) I think one can disprove that $f(N) = o(N)$ for all $N$ as follows. Let $\sigma_k(n)$ denote the sum of the divisors of $n$ excluding the top $k$ factors (or $0$, if the number of divisors $\tau(n)$ is at most $k$). It is a standard calculation (factoring $n=dm$) that $$ \sum_{n \leq x} \sigma(n) = \sum_{d \leq x} d \sum_{m \leq x/d} 1 \ll \sum_{d \leq x} x \ll x^2,$$ so $\sigma(n) = O(x)$ on the average. Suppose that we can more generally show $$ \sum_{n \leq x} \sigma_k(n) \ll x^2 / k^{1.1} \quad (1)$$ for all $k \geq 1$. By Markov's inequality and the union bound, and the summability of $\sum_k 1/k^{1.1}$, we conclude that for any $C_0$, the number of $n \leq x$ such that $\sigma_k(n) \geq C_0 x$ for at least one $k$ is at most $O( x / C_0 )$, hence we have $f(N) \leq x$ for at most $O(x/C_0)$ of the $N \in [C_0 x, 2C_0 x]$, which is enough to disprove $f(N) = o(N)$ by taking $C_0$ large enough. It remains to prove (1). In order for a factor $d$ of $n=dm$ to contribute to $\sigma_k(n)$, there must be at least $k$ factors of $dm$ that are at most $m$, so either $\tau(m) \gg k^{0.1}$ or $d$ has $\gg k^{0.9}$ factors of size at most $m$. The contribution of the first case is at most $$ \sum_{m \leq x} (\tau(m)/k^{0.1})^{100} \sum_{d \leq x/m} d \ll \frac{x^2}{k^{10}} \sum_m \frac{\tau(m)^{100}}{m^2} \ll \frac{x^2}{k^{10}}$$ which is acceptable (one could also have used the divisor bound $\tau(m) = m^{o(1)}$ here). The contribution of the second case is at most $$ \sum_{m \leq x} \sum_{d \leq x/m} d (\sum_{r \leq m: r|d} 1 / k^{0.9})^2$$ $$ \ll \sum_{m } \sum_{r_1,r_2 \leq m} \frac{x^2}{m^2 [r_1,r_2] k^{1.8}}$$ $$ \ll \sum_{m } \sum_{r_1,r_2} \frac{x^2}{m^{1.8} [r_1,r_2]^{1.1} k^{1.8}}$$ $$ \ll \frac{x^2}{k^{1.8}}$$ which is also acceptable (there is a lot of room to spare here ultimately coming from the fact that divisor functions are typically logarithmic in size, so presumably one could clean up this argument somehow; in particular, standard anatomy of integers heuristics predicts that (1) should in fact exhibit exponential decay in $k$).

That was so simple! The following is a trivial remark, but the claim that you prove:\[ \#\{N\in[C_0 x,\infty) : f(N)\leq x\} \ll \frac{x}{C_0}, \]also disproves any kind of statement $f(N)=o(N)$ for "almost all" $N$. Moreover, an immediate consequence is\[ \frac{\#\{N\in[1,X] : f(N)/N\leq \delta\}}{X} \ll \delta^2, \]which is perfectly in line with the above experimental results, like less than $6\%$ of the ratios $f(N)/N$ being less than $1/2$. Nice! Perhaps the only meaningful quantitative improvement would be to show that this quotient is $o(\delta^2)$, which seems to be achievable as well, but I need to think about it.

I think an estimation of second moments such as $\sum_{n \leq x} \sigma_k^2(n)$ might suffice to get the improved bound; the computations get messy, but on the other hand there is a lot of room and one can do rather crude estimations here. My intuition here is that it is only the really powerful numbers $n$ that can cause unusually small values of $f(N)/N$, and such numbers are asymptotically quite rare.

Makes sense. Also, now when I think about your proof, I am slightly confused why you even need to consider $\sigma_k$ (and sum the bounds in $k$) and not just use the trivial bound $\sum_{n\leq x}\sigma(n)\ll x^2$ (on the largest quantity dominating all $\sigma_k$). Edit: Nevermind this. For a moment I forgot what the problem was about. :-)

We need to sum in $k$, which is in principle unbounded (in particular, it can be large compared to $C_0$), because the definition of $f(N)$ does not specify $k$ in advance. More precisely, one has $f(N) = \inf_k f_k(N)$, where $f_k(N)$ is the least $n$ for which $\sigma_k(n)=N$ (or $\infty$ if no such $n$ exists). The "trivial" bound controls each individual event $f_k(N) \leq x$, but the event $f(N) \leq x$ is the union of all such events and so one needs to sum in $k$ if one wishes to use the union bound.
FWIW, GPT-5.5 Pro claims to improve this to $O(\delta^3)$ here.
Seems GPT-5.5 Pro was able to improve this to $O_k(\delta^k)$ for any fixed $k$ here.

Interesting, but why is this so complicated?! Some time ago I modified Terry's proof only slightly to prove the higher-moment estimate in the stronger form:\[ \sum_{n\leq x} \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} \Bigl(\frac{\sigma_j(n)}{n}\Bigr)^q \ll_q x. \]Just expand, flip the divisors, organize by the LCM, etc. The rest of the density $O(\delta^{q+1})$ argument is standard and I wrote it under [1054]. I didn't write anything on the forum, because this was just a minor modification of Terry's proof and I was trying to solve the limsup part of [1054]. Eventually I gave up on that and forgot. But nothing here escapes the AI predation. LOL
Unfortunately, as seen on many occasions, GPT very much overcomplicates things. It's something we are trying to work on from a prompt engineering angle.

Some perspectives on this which I think may be helpful. GPT doesn't "overcomplicate" things - that'd imply GPT has a simple idea in its mind which it then proceeds to write out in a complicated fashion. The truth is probably just that the idea in GPT's mind is about that complicated. It is common for an inexperienced student to derive things in a complicated fashion, which an expert can immediately "see through" as a short sequence of standard steps. GPT still lacks this experience relatively. That's why I don't think it's a prompt engineering problem to get it to write things out simply - because the root cause isn't that it's trying to *complicate* anything. When I do it, I always treat the problem as extra processing or extra work process: can it *identify* potential simplification and carry it out? This means we treat the simplification as *extra work* and not something that has not been done correctly from the start. Hope this helps.

I think the well-definedness issue can be closed, using the published constants from Helfgott and Rosser-Schoenfeld, I compiled a small verifier in this repo. Let \(d_1(m)<d_2(m)<\cdots\) be the divisors of \(m\). Say that \(n\) is represented if \(n=d_1(m)+\cdots+d_k(m)\) for some \(m,k\). The basic gadget is this: If \(M<p_1<\cdots<p_t\le X<M^2\) are primes, then for \(m=p_1\cdots p_t\), the divisors begin \(1,p_1,\ldots,p_t\), because every composite divisor is \(>M^2\). More generally, any subset sum \(N=p_{i_1}+\cdots+p_{i_s}\) of primes in \((M,X]\) gives a representation of \(N+1\), by taking \(m=p_{i_1}\cdots p_{i_s}\). The other elementary ingredient is the usual interval-extension trick. If subset sums cover \([C,U]\), and the next prime \(p\) satisfies \(p\le U-C+1\), then after adding \(p\) they cover \([C,U+p]\), since \([C,U]\) overlaps \([C+p,U+p]\). The verifier script checks these pieces (see the repo for instructions to run it and versioned output). 1. A direct \(Bq\) divisor-prefix search covers every \(6\le n\le10\,000\,000\), except \(7\). The remaining value is \(7=1+2+4\), represented by \(m=4\). 2. A first prime-window certificate with \(M=10\,000\) and \(X=99\,000\,000<M^2\) gives representations for every \(469\,616\le n\le 273\,803\,744\,799\,154.\) 3. A larger seed proves every integer in \([105\,000\,000,156\,000\,000]\) is a sum of distinct primes in \((20\,000\,000,40\,000\,000)\). The seed length is \(51\,000\,001\), while the next prime after \(40\, 000\,000\) is \(40\,000\,003\), so the interval-extension induction starts. After that it continues by Bertrand's postulate: once a prime \(p\) has been adjoined, the interval length is at least \(2p\), and the next prime is \(<2p\). Using Rosser--Schoenfeld bounds for \(\pi(x)\), the verifier lower-bounds the prime mass up to \(X=399\,000\,000\,000\,000<20\,000\,000^2\). This gives representations for every\[ 105\,000\,001\le n\le 1\,111\,351\,202\,532\,220\,892\,436\,000\,001. \]4. For the tail, I use an explicit consequence of Helfgott's ternary Goldbach proof: every odd \(N\ge 10^{27}\) is a sum of three distinct odd primes, each \(>N/(30000\log N)\). In Helfgott's Section 7 weighted odd-prime triple count, the lower bound is at least \(0.000422N^2\); we only need the weaker margin \(0.0002443N^2\). Using the published sup-norm bounds for the weights and Rosser-Schoenfeld's bound \(\sum_{n\le x}\Lambda(n)\le 1.03883x\), the verifier checks that triples with a coordinate \(\le N/(30000\log N)\) contribute \(<0.000178N^2\), and triples with a repeated coordinate contribute \(<10^{-20}N^2\). So at least one distinct prime triple remains with all three primes above \(N/(30000\log N)\). For even \(n\), write \(n-1=p+q+r\). Since \(pq>n-1>r\), the number \(m=pqr\) has initial divisors \(1,p,q,r\) and represents \(n\). For odd \(n\), choose a prime \(60000\log n<\ell<120000\log n\), and write \(n-1-\ell=p+q+r\). For \(n\ge 10^{27}+10^8\), the verifier checks the slack ensuring \(\ell<p\) and \(\ell p>r\). So \(m=\ell pqr\) has initial divisors \(1,\ell,p,q,r\) and represents \(n\). For odd \(n\), by Bertrand's postulate choose a prime \(60000\log n<\ell<120000\log n\), and write \(N=n-1-\ell\). Since \(\ell<10^8-1\) in this range, \(N\) is odd and at least \(10^{27}\), so the Helfgott consequence applies; write \(N=p+q+r\). For \(n\ge 10^{27}+10^8\), the verifier checks the slack ensuring \(\ell<p\) and \(\ell p>r\). So \(m=\ell pqr\) has initial divisors \(1,\ell,p,q,r\) and represents \(n\). So the covered intervals are: - \([6,10\,000\,000]\); - \([469\,616,273\,803\,744\,799\,154]\); - \([105\,000\,001, 1\,111\,351\,202\,532\,220\,892\,436\,000\,001]\); - \([10^{27}+10^8,\infty)\). They overlap, so every \(n\ge6\) is represented. Finally, \(2\) and \(5\) really are impossible. The first prefix is \(1\), and any longer prefix is at least \(1+2=3\), so \(2\) is impossible. For \(5\), a two-term prefix would have to be \(1+4\), but \(4\) cannot be the smallest divisor after \(1\); with at least three terms the sum is already at least \(6\). Also \(1,3,4\) are represented by \(m=1,2,3\). Thus the represented positive integers are exactly \(\mathbb N\setminus \{2,5\}\). References used: Helfgott, "The ternary Goldbach conjecture is true", Section 7. Rosser--Schoenfeld, "Approximate formulas for some functions of prime numbers".

This was disproved (quite strongly) by Terry Tao in the comments on [468], where he showed that the upper density of all $\{n\in\mathbb{N} : f(n)\leq \delta n\}$ is $\ll \delta^2$ (in a slightly different but equivalent formulation). Namely, denoting by $\sigma_k(n)$ the sum of divisors of $n$ excluding the $k$ largest ones, Terry proved that\[ \sum_{k\geq 0} \sum_{n\leq x} \sigma_k(n) \ll x^2. \]Therefore, for any $x,C>0$ we get \begin{align*} & \# \{N\geq Cx : f(N)\leq x\} \\ & \leq \# \{(n,k) : n\leq x, k\geq0, \sigma_k(n)\geq Cx\} \\ & = \sum_k \# \{ n\leq x : \sigma_k(n)\geq Cx\} \\ & \ll \frac{1}{Cx} \sum_{k\geq0} \sum_{n\leq x} \sigma_k(n) \ll \frac{x}{C}. \end{align*} For any $\delta,t>0$ we choose $C=1/(2\delta)$, $x=\delta t$ to obtain\[ \# \{ N\in [t/2, t] : f(N)\leq \delta N \} \ll \delta^2 t . \]Now split an arbitrary interval $[1,X]$ dyadically, i.e., apply the obtained inequality to $t=X/2^j$, $j=0,1,2,\ldots$ and sum in $j$ to finally get\[ \# \{ N\leq X : f(N)\leq \delta N \} \ll \delta^2 X . \] (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
I agree. Thing is, the function f itself has not been shown to be well-defined, which makes this problem open anyway.

This is essentially a duplicate of the last question of [468], meaning that the function $f$ defined here is obtained by merely shifting indices by 1 (since here we count 1 as a divisor). I gave some comments and experimental observations on the behavior of $f$ in the last paragraph under [468]. Either this problem is redundant, or the last question from [468] should be moved here, or (if nothing else) the two problems should link to each other.
Thanks, well spotted. I think I'll move the second question over here.

"Only true for almost all n" If the limsup would be $+\infty$, maybe there are non zero densities of n for which $f(n)>cn$ for $c$ in a certain regime So my question; it should have been clear that $f (n)=o(n)$ almost always? If one checks https://oeis.org/A167485/a167485.png, it seems $f(n)=o(n)$ might be false, since the distribution does not change a lot over these intervals (of course, guessing from small values is always dangerous)
